item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was a wonderful story and the last interview honestly made me tear up a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was marvelous, no complaints. :D In particular, I like how the 'wave' of people slowly forgetting them would have spread at the rate of a worldwide hit single.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wholly agree. Interviews, especially of this length, should mainly exist to provide new information to the reader, not just be something we already know. By the end it felt like i read a tale more than an actual article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this entry. In addition to the heartbreak and melancholy of the four most famous men in the world hitting instant obscurity through no fault of their own, I love their interactions with the SCP agents. It would be easy to just go have the SCP take the typical "overly formal, dehumanising and cold" approach, but it's the notes of kindness we get from them that makes it even more tragic. On a personal level, the Foundation sympathises with these men — but it's got a bigger role to play that means that institutionally, that sympathy can only ever go so far.

If I had one criticism:

+ And it's a minor one
- (Unless you're a massive pedant like me)

I'd suggest that there are a few points that suggest that Tyrannosaurus Flex's journey to the top was, even by the standards of rock and roll, a little… rushed. You state that they formed in 1971, and yet by 1980 they're not only the top of the world, but they've recorded 13 albums (that's almost one and a half a year). By contrast, the Beatles — the band they surpass — formed in 1960 (or 1958 if we want to be really specific) and spent about two(-four) years playing in dingy little venues in Liverpool and Hamburg before releasing their first hit single, so unless Tyrannosaurus Flex hit it big immediately after forming they'd have had to cram thirteen albums into, what, six years? Seven or eight at most? While also touring, promoting and basically living like rock gods?

While, granted, the Beatles also recorded the same amount of albums in roughly the same amount of time, it's also worth noting that the process of making their albums in the early sixties was a lot more rushed than it was in the late sixties, and that albums would be in the seventies. "Please Please Me" (1963) was recorded in a single day, while "Abbey Road" (1969) took six months. And for comparison sake, if we only count their seventies releases Pink Floyd released four albums across the entire 1970s and both Led Zeppelin and the Stones only recorded six. And all of those bands had been going since the mid-to-late sixties.

I'm being pedantic, granted, but the numbering doesn't really sit quite right with me. Even if it was along the lines of them having released their first number one in 1971, that would seem a little more plausible to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My headcanonical Foundation would probably be kinder to an anomaly like this,but I nevertheless still found this depiction affecting, so +1 for aiming for emotions that Skipton doesn't generally.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Didn't expect to like it this much when I read the description, but hey, nicely done.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Novote because I agree with the people who dislike it and I kinda wish all that silly introductory stuff wasn't added.

I understand the agent, but still.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was… Heartbreaking. Excellent work. I got a little nervous when I saw the new header, but I think now that it really adds something to it.

"We were bigger than God." To have your name and identity stripped away in the course of one minute is so mind-numbing.

+freaking 1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love your work, Mortos. This is only raising the bar for us mediocre authors. Well put together skip all around.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the woman conducting the final interview did not tell SCP-3933-B what happened to his parents because his parents likely forgot him too, just like everyone else.

To me, keeping the remaining band members in custody forever does not seem like something the Foundation would not do. Of course they would. Remember, their primary goal is the preservation of normalcy. Letting the band members outside freely would not be harmful to humanity, but it would pose a significant danger to normalcy. Even if it is just some people seeing a sandwich disappear, as you put it.

I do agree it is unrealistic that the Foundation would never do any kind of research involving them playing instruments though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is so sad. Especially the last interview.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, please leave it to the tagging team.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good job Mortos! I hope you don't mind, I took the liberty of tagging your page. If leaving it untagged was your intention, disregard!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with this, particularly the comments on the format and the Foundation jail. I found using this SCP to tell the 'jail forever' story particularly silly, because this is almost designed to be harmless from both a danger and secrecy POV - the whole point is that nobody notices them. The worst thing that could happen is somebody sees a sandwich disappear or something like that, and it would be trivial to get the band members to not do that stuff if it was their only ticket outside every month or so. There are also plenty of places where people would not have ever heard their music and be unaffected.

(and what possible reason could they have for not telling the guy what happened to his parents? Are they part of a different SCP now?)

Even if one decides the Foundation is 100% evil and cares not a whit for the wellbeing of those in its custody, stating that they never a) had any instruments and b) never saw each other again implies the Foundation also never bothered to do a single bit of experimental research on this - again, on something pretty harmless relative to other things they investigate (sure, you might have to rotate out some staff who got exposed)

Edit: it crossed my mind that the Foundation treating these guys badly could be a result of most staff having forgotten about them. I don't get that from the article, but colour me impressed if that was intentional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This feels like something straight out of the Twilight Zone. Good job!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This doesn't really work for me. One interview was essentially "man, you sure are mean, Dr. Foundationdoctor," which has already been done a thousand times on this site (this was also present in the other interviews as well). One interview contains a 400-word recap of information that has already been disclosed to the reader.

I had no interest in these characters because it just felt like more of the same "I used to be a normal person (or in this case, not), but now here I am in a cell why is this happening to me" stuff that we read with almost every single humanoid article that includes a log. There's no real feeling of the meteoric rise and equally meteoric fall of the band. Just "they were big, then forgotten." I feel like there is a lot of ground to explore the ideas you're putting forth in this, but I don't think it's done in a particularly compelling way.

Also, as a side note, Tyranosaurus Flex is a distractingly dumb name for a glam rock band. It reads too much like the actual glam rock band T. Rex. Maybe that's what you were going for, but it's like having a fake British Invasion band called "the Beedles" or something.

The final bit is by far the strongest, because it actually shows a development of the character from anger to acceptance, which is kind of cool. If you focused more on the process whereby their anger becomes acceptance both of their containment and irrelevance, that would be something I would like to read. As is, -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At the beginning I was unsure about this since I found it similar to that SCP which makes people forget about it once encountered. But I've found interesting the twist on this one. And the interviews at the end really sold the deal for me. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find opening an article to almost a page's worth of not-article (at least on my monitor) kind of exhausting. The contents of the BY THE ORDER OF etc. image is pretty much conveyed through the screening test, since they wouldn't be screening unless there was something to screen for, and I guess the rest of the formatting doesn't do much for me personally.

I ended up being preemptively dissatisfied before reading the article because of all that, so I'm leaving it at a novote for now, but I really don't think the large body of stuff at the top serves the rest of it well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm also really tired of cute code phrases:

It's a lyric from the song it's actively testing for exposure to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

I've been refraining from saying anything about the excess of formatting recently, but this is becoming prevalent enough that I feel the need to mention it.

Whenever I see the trademark Kaktus format (and I'm not really wild about seeing what used to be a distinctive look becoming franchised out), I'm expecting something especially noteworthy and dense, narrative-wise. Something to justify fucking with the established format. This didn't really rise to the occasion for me in that regard.

I'm also really tired of cute code phrases:

I want your body but your soul makes me sick.

I feel like this is becoming standard practice for passcodes and corrupted file names and everywhere else people can get away with it. To me, it lowers the articles it appears in. Semi-profundity isn't usually doing the attached article any favors.

Additionally, most of the first interview, rather than serving as the needed characterization that I'm guessing you were going for, just restates what we already know from the description. For that reason, it drags and wastes precious attention.

The core of your story is what happens to the band after it releases its song and its subsequent containment. This story doesn't work for me on two levels:

If what you're going for is pathos (which is what I'm assuming you're going for), this isn't really communicating it very well. Your bigger-than-the-Beatles band suddenly having their fame stripped from them would be far more affecting if it were given more room to explore in a more normal, relatable setting. The story of a man suddenly forgotten having to live the rest of his life as best he can would be far more of a struggle than what you've got going here, which is just the band being locked up forever. And if you're going to try to make me as a reader invest myself in that story, you're going to need to do a lot more work than a brief introduction prior to a suicide and then two more vignettes that don't really tell us a whole lot.

There's also the so-very-tired "Foundation Jail" trope that's played exactly straight. I'm not convinced that the Foundation, an organization used to devising creative solutions to problems that most people don't even know exist, resorts to "jail forever" at the drop of a hat for anyone associated with an anomalous phenomenon. It doesn't make a whole lot of sense on a logical level, and that's working against taking this narrative seriously. There's also the whole angst over referring to someone by their name here, which never made a whole lot of sense either to begin with and has been pounded into the ground in hundreds of interviews already on the site. And speaking of nonsensical reactions, the pointless cruelty of the Foundation here is immersion-breakingly dumb. Why on earth would they not at least let a group of musicians have instruments during their life sentence? Surely they would be actively researching how these guys did what they did in the first place; at the very least you can imagine them running experiments with individual band members playing their various instruments. The denial of basic human interactions and expression that people in actual prisons routinely get doesn't have any ring of truth to it. This, combined with all the other problems inherent in all the other stories which ask us to feel sorry for something in containment, is really cutting off any chance this story had at the knees, for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wonderful job Mortos, seeing their descent from stars to broken old men was haunting.

item.title: Staff Post - Closed, do not respond
item.content:encoded:
Being the fastest idiot isn't something to celebrate. Don't do "lol, first" posts. Comment on the article, or don't comment.

Do not respond to this post if you are not staff.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Mr toribo,

Don't make spammy, no-content posts, please.

item.title: Re: Wow
item.content:encoded: Very fast good job

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
After the ridiculousness of the last article I posted, I felt the need to write something a little more serious, so this is basically the exact opposite of my previous article.

Thanks to the following for reading it in draft:
SunnyClockwork
Tufto
DarkStuff
NineVolt
Hippo
djkaktus
Mendelssohn

Edit: Oh, and the format is obviously inspired by djkaktus'!

Find more of my stuff here.

Image credits:
All images are created directly by me or edited from photos I took myself, with the following exceptions:

The first part of the 3 meme images is edited from this photo: https://www.flickr.com/photos/tylerchristian/6061549815/in/pool-creative_commons-_free_pictures

(the other two photos in the meme triptych are mine.)

The concert photo is edited from this https://pixabay.com/en/concert-performance-audience-336695/

item.title: Wow
item.content:encoded: I,m realy fast :D
